title: Entryway Storage Bench
summary: Sit down, take your boots off, put them underneath. Painted poplar over a plywood carcass.
description:
A bench with cubbies underneath solves the pile of shoes by the front door. It's plywood casework with a solid-wood face frame — the cheap, fast, entirely respectable way to build furniture that gets painted.

The seat is the one part that needs to be solid and stiff. Everyone will sit on it, and some of them will drop onto it.

Poplar is the classic paint-grade hardwood: cheap, machines cleanly, takes paint beautifully. Don't stain it — poplar's green-and-purple streaks look strange under clear finish.

tools.note: Or a track saw, for breaking down full plywood sheets before they go near the table saw.
Fast, strong, hidden joinery for plywood casework. Exactly what it's good at.

materials.name: Birch plywood, 3/4", 4x8
materials.note: Cabinet-grade. Construction ply has voids that show as holes in your edges.

materials.name: Poplar, 8/4, for the seat
materials.note: Solid and thick. The seat carries the load.

materials.name: Primer + latex enamel paint
materials.note: Enamel, not wall paint. This surface gets kicked by boots.

cutList.note: Creates three cubbies. Each ends up ~14" wide — enough for a pair of boots.
Glued up from narrower boards. Overhangs 1" front and sides.

steps.title: Break down the plywood
steps.body:
Cut the sheets into manageable pieces with a circular saw and a straight-edge before you try to wrestle a full 4x8 sheet across a table saw. Fighting a full sheet alone is how people get hurt and how cuts go crooked.

Then cut to final size on the table saw.

steps.title: Assemble the carcass
steps.body:
Pocket-hole the bottom to the sides and the two dividers, screwing from the underside and inside faces where the holes won't show.

Add the back — glue and screw it. It squares the case and stops it from racking when someone drops onto the seat.

Check the diagonals before the glue grabs.

steps.title: Glue up the seat
steps.body:
Edge-glue poplar to make a 16-1/2" x 49" slab, 1-1/2" thick. Alternate the growth rings, use cauls, clamp it flat.

This is the only structural solid-wood part. Take your time.

steps.title: Build and attach the face frame
steps.body:
Pocket-hole the poplar stiles and rails into a frame, then glue and clamp it onto the front of the plywood carcass.

The face frame is what hides the plywood edges and makes it read as furniture instead of a box.

steps.title: Attach the seat
steps.body:
Screw up through the top of the carcass into the seat, using **oversized clearance holes** so the solid seat can move seasonally without splitting.

A 16"-wide poplar slab moves about 1/8" across the year. Give it room.

steps.title: Fill, sand, prime, paint
steps.body:
Fill every screw hole and every plywood edge void. Sand it all flush at 150, then 180.

Prime. **Then sand the primer** — this is the step that separates a paint job that looks professional from one that looks like a weekend project. Primer raises grain; knock it back with 220.

Two coats of enamel. Let it cure a few days before you load it with boots — latex enamel takes a week to reach full hardness, and boots on soft paint leave marks forever.
